prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: Bats can detect only objects that are completely still, never moving ones.
choices.rationale: Bats detect motion through shifts in the returning sound.

choices.text: Bats produce sounds that humans can easily hear during the night.
choices.rationale: The calls are often too high for humans to hear.

choices.text: Bats perceive their surroundings in the dark by interpreting echoes of their own calls.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage explains how bats build a sound-based picture from echoes.

choices.text: Bats rely chiefly on their sharp eyesight to hunt insects after dark.
choices.rationale: The passage describes hunting by sound, not eyesight.

explanation: The passage centers on echolocation as a sound-built perception of the world.

stimulus.title: Seeing With Sound
stimulus.text: A bat hunting in pitch darkness does not bump into things. It calls out, often at pitches too high for humans to hear, and listens for the echoes that bounce back. From the delay between call and echo, the bat judges how far away an object is; from subtle shifts in the returning sound, it can tell whether the object is moving toward it or away. A moth's fluttering wing returns a different echo than a still leaf. So precise is this sense that some bats can snatch an insect from the air in total darkness. The world the bat perceives is built entirely of sound, a picture painted with echoes.

prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: Honeybees locate rich patches of flowers almost entirely by chance and luck.
choices.rationale: The dance communicates location, not luck.

choices.text: The honeybee's waggle dance is a symbolic code for a food source's direction and distance.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage explains how the dance encodes direction and distance.

choices.text: Honeybees actually speak to one another using a range of distinct vocal sounds.
choices.rationale: The passage stresses communication 'without sound.'

choices.text: The honeybee's waggle dance carries no consistent or reliable meaning at all.
choices.rationale: The dance reliably encodes specific information.

explanation: The passage frames the waggle dance as a precise symbolic language.

stimulus.title: Dances in the Dark
stimulus.text: A foraging honeybee that finds a rich patch of flowers cannot simply tell the hive where to go. Instead, it dances. On the vertical comb, in darkness, the bee runs a straight line while waggling its body, then loops back to repeat the move. The angle of that straight run, measured against the pull of gravity, encodes the direction of the flowers relative to the sun. The length of the run signals distance. Watching bees crowd around and follow, researchers realized they were seeing a kind of symbolic language, one that passes precise information without sight, sound, or a single spoken word.

prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: Fog nets can supply useful drinking water in suitable coastal areas, within real limits.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage presents fog harvesting as useful where conditions fit, while noting limits.

choices.text: Fog almost never holds enough moisture to be worth trying to collect at all.
choices.rationale: The passage says nets can collect many liters.

choices.text: Fog nets can completely replace every other source of water in any location.
choices.rationale: The passage calls it a supplement, not a universal replacement.

choices.text: Harvesting water from fog demands complex machinery and a steady power supply.
choices.rationale: It relies on gravity and simple mesh, not complex machinery.

explanation: The balanced claim is that fog nets work well in the right setting but are not a cure-all.

stimulus.title: Catching Water From Air
stimulus.text: In a few dry coastal regions, drinking water can be harvested from fog. Tall mesh nets are strung across hillsides where moist air rolls in from the sea. As the fog drifts through, droplets cling to the mesh, merge, and trickle down into troughs and storage tanks. A single large net can collect many liters on a good day, enough to supplement a village's supply. The method is not a cure-all: it works only where fog is frequent and winds are steady, and the nets need upkeep. Yet in the right place, it turns a passing cloud into a dependable resource, using little more than gravity and patience.

prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: Kelp forests are rich but fragile habitats that depend on a balance of species.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage presents kelp forests as rich habitats whose health rests on species balance.

choices.text: Sea otters are harmful pests that destroy the kelp forests they live in.
choices.rationale: Otters protect the kelp; their loss lets urchins destroy it.

choices.text: Giant kelp grows so slowly that it can take many years to reach the surface.
choices.rationale: The passage says kelp grows astonishingly fast, not slowly.

choices.text: Kelp forests are found mainly in warm, sandy waters near tropical beaches.
choices.rationale: The passage places these forests off cool, rocky coasts.

explanation: The passage frames kelp forests as productive yet fragile, balanced ecosystems.

stimulus.title: Forests Beneath the Waves
stimulus.text: Off some cool, rocky coasts grow forests that no hiker will ever walk through. Giant kelp, anchored to the seafloor, can stretch dozens of meters toward the surface, swaying in the current like trees in wind. These kelp forests shelter fish, snails, and otters, much as a woodland shelters birds and deer. The kelp grows astonishingly fast, sometimes half a meter in a single day, drinking sunlight near the surface. But the forest is fragile. Where otters vanish, sea urchins multiply and graze the kelp down to bare rock, leaving a barren the divers call an urchin desert. A single missing animal can unmake a whole forest.

prompt: Which choice best states the main idea of the passage?

choices.text: Coral grows faster than trees and so makes a more accurate calendar.
choices.rationale: The passage compares coral to trees but never claims it grows faster.

choices.text: Researchers prefer studying coral because it is easier than studying trees.
choices.rationale: No claim of relative ease over tree study is made.

choices.text: Coral bands preserve a long, readable record of past ocean conditions.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage centers on how coral bands record and reveal past conditions.

choices.text: Warm water is the only factor that determines how thick a coral band grows.
choices.rationale: Chemistry and season both matter; warmth is not called the only factor.

explanation: The passage frames the coral as a centuries-long record that researchers learn to read.

stimulus.title: The Coral That Keeps Time
stimulus.text: A coral skeleton grows in fine bands, much like the rings of a tree. In bright, warm months the coral lays down thick layers; in cooler, dimmer months the layers thin. By slicing a coral and counting these bands, researchers can read a record of past seasons stretching back centuries. Even better, the chemistry trapped in each band hints at the water's temperature when it formed. So a single old coral can serve as a kind of diary, written not in words but in stone, recording conditions long before anyone thought to measure them. The challenge is learning to read a language no one designed to be read.

prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: Wind is the only means by which any plant can spread its seeds.
choices.rationale: The passage describes wind, hooks, and fruit, not wind alone.

choices.text: Plants are able to uproot themselves and walk to find better ground.
choices.rationale: The passage opens by noting a plant cannot walk.

choices.text: Seeds always grow best directly beneath the shade of the parent plant.
choices.rationale: The passage says seeds need to leave the parent's shadow.

choices.text: Plants have evolved varied methods to disperse their seeds away from the parent.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage surveys several strategies plants use to move their seeds.

explanation: The passage's focus is the variety of seed-dispersal strategies.

stimulus.title: Travelers Without Legs
stimulus.text: A plant cannot walk, yet its seeds must somehow leave the shadow of the parent and find open ground. Evolution has answered this need with a surprising range of tricks. Some seeds grow papery wings and spin away on the wind like tiny helicopters. Others wear hooks that snag the fur of passing animals, hitching a ride for miles. Still others hide inside sweet fruit, counting on an animal to eat them and carry them off, depositing them far away. Each method is a kind of bargain struck with the world around the plant. The seed that travels farthest is often the one with the best chance to grow where the parent's shade cannot reach.

prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: Voting has always been conducted privately throughout recorded history.
choices.rationale: The passage says voting was long public, not always private.

choices.text: Public voting by show of hands gave ordinary workers the most power.
choices.rationale: Public voting invited pressure on workers, reducing their power.

choices.text: The secret ballot freed voters to choose without fear of pressure or retaliation.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage explains how private ballots protected voters from pressure.

choices.text: Reformers opposed the printed ballot as an unnecessary change.
choices.rationale: Reformers pushed for the printed ballot, not against it.

explanation: The passage's point is that the secret ballot protected the ordinary voter's free choice.

stimulus.title: The Secret Ballot
stimulus.text: It is easy to assume voting has always been private, but for a long time it was not. In many places people voted aloud or by a show of hands, in full view of employers, landlords, and neighbors. That openness invited pressure: a worker might think twice before voting against a boss who could see the choice. Reformers pushed for a printed ballot, marked alone and dropped in a box, so that no one need fear retaliation for an honest vote. The change seemed minor, a mere matter of paper, yet it shifted real power toward the ordinary voter, who could now decide without an audience.

prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: Canal locks let boats overcome changes in elevation, opening inland trade.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage explains how locks conquer elevation and thereby expand trade.

choices.text: Rivers were once the fastest possible way to move goods over long distances.
choices.rationale: The passage does not rank rivers as the fastest route.

choices.text: Boats could always travel freely inland long before the lock was invented.
choices.rationale: The passage says elevation once blocked inland travel.

choices.text: Locks required rare and costly materials that few towns could ever afford.
choices.rationale: The passage stresses humble means, not rare materials.

explanation: The lock's purpose and its effect on inland commerce are the passage's focus.

stimulus.title: Stairs for Boats
stimulus.text: Rivers do not run level, and for centuries that simple fact blocked boats from traveling far inland. The lock changed things. A lock is a chamber with gates at each end; a boat enters, the gates close, and water is let in or drained out until the boat rises or falls to the next stretch of river. String several locks together, and a vessel can climb a hillside the way a person climbs a staircase. The engineering is humble, relying on nothing more exotic than gravity and patience, yet it knit distant towns into trading partners and turned shallow rivers into highways of commerce.
